title: The Weakest Disciple
content:
The morning bell of the Seven Stars Sect echoed across the mountain peaks, its deep resonance carrying through the mist that clung to every pine and pagoda.

Lin Feng was already awake. He had been awake for three hours, in fact, crouched in the disciple training yard long before the first ray of sun had breached the eastern ridge. His robes were soaked through with dew and sweat, and his breath came in ragged bursts. A pile of splintered practice swords lay at his feet.

"Still here, trash?"

The voice belonged to Zhao Wei, an inner disciple who passed through the outer courtyard each morning on his way to the spirit spring. He wore robes of deep blue silk, embroidered with the silver cloud patterns that marked his status. His cultivation base sat solidly at the fourth level of Qi Refinement — nothing special by inner sect standards, but light-years beyond anything Lin Feng could dream of.

Lin Feng didn't answer. He'd learned long ago that words only made things worse. He kept swinging the broken sword, moving through the Seven Forms he'd practiced ten thousand times. His muscles screamed. His meridians burned with the familiar ache of qi depletion. But the movements were correct. The forms were perfect.

Too bad perfect forms meant nothing when your spiritual roots were garbage.

Zhao Wei snorted and moved on. Lin Feng waited until the sound faded before allowing himself to collapse onto the dirt. His chest heaved. Black spots danced in his vision.

The diagnosis from the Sect Physician still echoed in his memory, even two years later: "Mortal-grade roots. The weakest we've ever recorded. You will never break through to Qi Refinement."

And yet here he was. Still training. Still swinging. Still hoping.

Something warm pulsed against his chest.

Lin Feng frowned and reached inside his robes. His fingers closed around the jade pendant — a plain, unremarkable piece of green stone that his mother had given him the day before she died. It hung from a frayed red cord that he'd never once removed, not even to bathe.

The pendant was glowing.

A faint, pulsing light emanated from deep within the stone, the color of green leaves in spring rain. It was warm against his palm, warmer than it should have been, warmer than anything had felt in a very long time.

"What in the—"

The world tilted. Lin Feng's vision went white. He felt himself falling, tumbling through a space that had no direction, no time, no boundary. And then—

"So. You're the one who awakened me."

The voice was ancient. It spoke without sound, resonating directly within his consciousness, each syllable carrying the weight of millennia.

"A mortal with broken meridians. Delicious irony."

"Who are you?" Lin Feng gasped. He was no longer in the training yard. He stood — or seemed to stand — in a vast hall of white jade. Pillars rose into infinity. A throne sat at the far end, carved from a single piece of obsidian that absorbed all light. And on that throne, a figure.

"I am the Sword God Xuan Yuan. And you, little mortal, have just become my inheritor."

Lin Feng's knees buckled. The Sword God. The legend who had once split the heavens with a single strike. The being whose name was spoken only in whispers by the oldest sect masters.

"Why me?" Lin Feng managed.

"Because," the Sword God said, a cold smile playing at his lips, "you are the weakest. No one will suspect you. No one will watch you. And when you rise, they will never see it coming."

The vision shattered. Lin Feng found himself back in the training yard, still on his knees, the morning bell still echoing in the distance. But something was different. His body felt lighter. The ache in his meridians had vanished. And when he closed his eyes, he could see them — pathways of light running through his body like rivers of stars.

For the first time in his life, Lin Feng could sense qi.

The Sword God's final words lingered in his mind: "Tomorrow, we begin. Rest tonight. You will need it."

Lin Feng looked up at the peak of Seven Stars Mountain, where the sect's grand hall blazed with the light of a thousand spirit lamps. For two years, he had been the weakest. The laughingstock. The mortal among immortals.

Tomorrow, everything would change.

title: Jade Pendant's Secret
content:
Lin Feng did not sleep that night. He couldn't. Every time he closed his eyes, the Sword God's voice echoed in his mind, and the rivers of starlight behind his eyelids pulsed with an alien intensity that made his heart race.

He sat cross-legged on his cot in the outer disciple dormitory, the jade pendant cradled in his palms. In the faint moonlight filtering through the paper window, it looked exactly as it always had — a plain green stone, unremarkable, the kind of trinket a poor farmer's wife might wear to market.

"Are you still there?" Lin Feng whispered.

"I am always here," the voice responded immediately. "This pendant was forged from my soul essence. As long as you wear it, I cannot leave. Whether that is a blessing or a curse depends entirely on you."

"Why did you choose me?"

"I didn't. The pendant did. It was programmed to awaken when it detected the weakest cultivator within a hundred li. After three thousand years, you were finally weak enough."

Lin Feng wasn't sure whether to feel honored or insulted. "So I'm just... the lowest bidder?"

"Precisely." The Sword God's mental laugh was dry, like autumn leaves. "But you misunderstand, boy. Being weak is not a flaw. It's a blank canvas. Every meridian you've tried to open and failed? They're not broken. They're waiting. A strong cultivator's meridians are rigid, set in their ways. Yours are... malleable."

"Shaped into what?"

"Into something new. Something I spent the last thousand years of my life designing. A cultivation path that no one has ever walked before."

Lin Feng's breath caught. A new cultivation path? That was impossible. The paths were fixed — Qi Refinement, Foundation Establishment, Core Formation, Nascent Soul. To create a new path was to defy the Heavenly Dao itself.

"Is that even possible?"

"I was the Sword God. I did many impossible things. Now listen carefully — your first task is simple. Forget everything the Seven Stars Sect taught you. Their methods are garbage. True power comes from within. Your body already contains all the qi you will ever need — it is simply locked away, waiting for the right key."

"The pendant is the key?"

"The pendant is a map. You are the key. Your weakness is your strength. Every failure, every humiliation, every moment of despair you've experienced — all of it has created fractures in your spiritual foundation. And through those fractures, I will teach you to draw power."

Before Lin Feng could respond, the pendant flared with heat. A current of energy — not qi, something older, something rawer — surged through his body. His meridians screamed, but this time not with failure. They were opening. Expanding. Rewriting themselves according to a pattern that existed nowhere in the known cultivation world.

When the surge subsided, Lin Feng looked down at his hands. They were glowing faintly, a pale silver light that seemed to come from the very bones.

"You have opened your first meridian. Not according to the Seven Stars method. According to mine. The Celestial Blade Meridian."

The Sword God's voice grew cold. "This is not a gift, boy. This is a debt. I am investing in you. One day, I will collect."

"What do you want from me?"

"I want to live again. And to do that, I need a body. A body strong enough to contain a god's soul. You will become that body — or you will die trying. There is no third option."

The first bell of dawn rang across Seven Stars Mountain.

"Get up. It's time for your first real lesson."

title: First Sword Form
content:
The outer disciple training yard was empty when Lin Feng arrived, still an hour before sunrise. The mist was thicker than usual, swirling around his ankles like ghost-silk. Perfect.

"Hold out your hand," the Sword God commanded.

Lin Feng obeyed. Knowledge flooded into his mind — not words, but muscle memory. The exact angle of a wrist. The distribution of weight across the balls of the feet. The precise path a blade must trace through the air to part qi rather than cut flesh.

"This is the First Form of the Celestial Blade: Dawn Pierces Mist. If you cannot master this, you will never master anything."

Lin Feng grabbed a wooden practice sword. It was heavier than the ones he usually used — a training weapon for inner disciples, reinforced with spirit iron. Normally, he couldn't even lift it. Today, it felt like a feather.

He raised the sword. The movement was not his own — his body was being guided by the implanted memory, a ghost's hands on his shoulders. The blade cut upward at a forty-five degree angle, then swept left in a crescent arc.

"Again. Your right shoulder is too tense."

He tried again. The sword traced the same path, but this time a faint shiver ran through the blade. It wasn't qi. It wasn't anything Lin Feng recognized. But it was something.

"Better. That is the secret of the Celestial Blade — you do not project qi outward like other techniques. You channel it inward, compressing it into the blade itself. Every strike contains the force of your entire body, concentrated into a single edge."

Lin Feng swung again. And again. Time lost meaning. The sun rose, painting the eastern peaks gold. Other disciples trickled into the yard, but he didn't hear their whispers. He was somewhere else entirely — deep inside himself, where the only things that existed were the sword and the voice.

"Stop."

Lin Feng froze mid-swing. The wooden sword in his hands was... different. A hairline crack ran from hilt to tip, glowing faintly with silver light.

"You channeled too much. The spirit iron couldn't handle your output. That is... unexpected."

The crack widened. The sword was coming apart from the inside. Lin Feng hurled it away. It spun through the air and embedded itself in the stone wall of the training yard, sinking a full foot into solid rock.

The other disciples stared.

"Hey! Lin Feng!" Zhao Wei was striding across the yard, his face twisted with confusion. "What trick is this? Did you bribe a talisman master?"

"I didn't—"

"Don't lie to me, trash!" Zhao Wei grabbed him by the collar. "I've known you for two years. You couldn't cut a blade of grass without crying. What did you do?"

The Sword God's voice came as a cold whisper: "Kill him."

Lin Feng's blood ran cold. "No!"

"He is an obstacle. A bully. He will never stop unless you make him stop."

"No!" Lin Feng said aloud, to Zhao Wei, to himself, to the cultivation world that demanded blood for every step forward.

The Sword God's consciousness pressed against his will like a mountain. But Lin Feng had spent two years being crushed by mountains. He knew how to bend without breaking.

The pressure released. When the Sword God spoke again, his voice held something new. Respect.

"Interesting. Perhaps you are not as weak as I thought."

Zhao Wei, oblivious to the battle that had just taken place inside Lin Feng's head, shoved him backward. "Whatever trick you're using, it won't last. Tricks never do."

He spat on the ground and walked away.

Lin Feng stood in the training yard, breathing hard, surrounded by staring disciples and a wooden sword embedded in stone.

"Well done," the Sword God said quietly. "You passed the first test — and it wasn't the sword form. Now come. We have much more work to do."

title: Elder's Suspicion
content:
News traveled fast in the Seven Stars Sect. By noon, every disciple in the outer court had heard about Lin Feng's impossible feat with the training sword. By evening, the rumors had reached the inner court. By the following morning, Elder Xu himself had summoned Lin Feng to the Disciplinary Hall.

Elder Xu sat at the head of the hall like a vulture on a corpse, his thin frame wrapped in robes of midnight silk. His eyes — pale gray, almost colorless — studied Lin Feng with the detached curiosity of a scholar examining a particularly interesting insect.

"I have read your file," Elder Xu said, his voice soft and terrifying. "Mortal-grade roots. Zero cultivation progress in two years. Latrine duty for insubordination. And yesterday, you threw a training sword through a stone wall."

Lin Feng said nothing. The Sword God's advice had been specific: "Say as little as possible. Elder Xu is a Nascent Soul cultivator — he cannot sense my presence, but he can sense that something is wrong."

"Nothing to say?" Elder Xu leaned forward. "In my two hundred years of life, I have seen many strange things. Demonic possessions. Forbidden techniques. Artifacts left over from the Godfall Era." His eyes narrowed. "Which one are you?"

"None of them, Elder Xu." Lin Feng kept his head bowed. "I don't know what happened. I was training. The sword broke."

"The sword broke." Elder Xu gestured, and an attendant brought forward the shattered remains. The wooden blade had splintered into a dozen pieces, each one still glowing with faint silver residue. The spirit iron wrapping had bent outward, like the petals of a metal flower that had bloomed from within.

"This is not qi," Elder Xu said quietly. "This is something else entirely. Something I do not recognize. And that, boy, is what frightens me."

"Give him a partial truth," the Sword God whispered. "Tell him you found a cultivation manual in the abandoned pavilion on the eastern peak."

"Elder Xu," Lin Feng said, "may I speak honestly?"

"Please do."

"I found something. Three months ago, when I was assigned to sweep the abandoned pavilion on the eastern peak, I found an old cultivation manual hidden under a loose floorboard. It described a different way of cultivating. A way that doesn't use qi. I was desperate. So I tried it."

"And where is this manual now?"

"I burned it. After yesterday, I was afraid. I thought I might have done something wrong."

Elder Xu was silent for a long moment. Then he laughed — a dry, rattling sound. "You burned it. Of course you did." He stood. "Very well, boy. You are free to go. But know this: I will be watching you. If you show any further signs of unusual development, we will have another conversation. And it will not end so pleasantly."

Lin Feng bowed and retreated from the hall. His heart was pounding so hard he could feel it in his temples.

"That was close," the Sword God said. "He doesn't believe you. But for now, he has no proof. We have bought ourselves time. Use it well."

"How much time?"

"A few months, perhaps. Long enough for you to reach Foundation Establishment. After that, no one in this sect will be able to threaten us. Not even Elder Xu."

Lin Feng walked back to the outer disciple dormitory as the sun set behind Seven Stars Mountain. He had told a lie that might cost him his life. He had defied a Nascent Soul elder. And somewhere inside him, a dead god was laughing.

title: Night Training
content:
For the next week, Lin Feng trained only at night.

It was safer that way. During the day, Elder Xu's spies — and Lin Feng had no doubt they existed — watched his every move. But the sect's surveillance protocol had a blind spot: the hours between midnight and dawn, when even the most dedicated watchers succumbed to sleep.

Their training ground was a hidden cave halfway up the eastern cliff face, accessible only by a narrow ledge that Lin Feng had discovered during his latrine duty days. The cave was damp and cold, its walls covered in phosphorescent moss that cast everything in an eerie blue glow.

"Tonight," the Sword God announced, "we will attempt something that has not been done in three thousand years. I am going to teach you to manifest a weapon from your soul."

Lin Feng stared at the ghostly figure beside him. "You can do that?"

"I can do that. You cannot. Yet. That is what we are here to learn."

The theory was deceptively simple. Every cultivator's soul contained an imprint of their most fundamental nature — a color, a temperature, an emotion. For those who walked the path of the blade, the imprint took a more specific form. A sword made not of steel but of spirit.

"Close your eyes. Find the place inside you where the silver light lives."

Lin Feng closed his eyes. The silver threads of his newly opened meridians pulsed gently beneath his skin.

"Good. Now draw them together. Imagine you are weaving them into a single cord of pure, compressed will."

It was like trying to braid lightning. Each time Lin Feng gathered a thread, another slipped free. Sweat beaded on his forehead.

"You are fighting yourself. Stop fighting. The threads are part of you. Let them flow."

Lin Feng exhaled. And something shifted. The silver threads stopped writhing and began to move together, flowing like water into a shared channel. They spiraled inward, compressing, condensing, until Lin Feng's entire being was focused on a single point of light in the center of his chest.

"Open your hands."

He did. And in his palms, shimmering like a heat mirage, lay a sword.

It was not a physical sword — not yet. It was a sword of pure concept, a possibility given form. But it was there, glowing with the same silver light, and when Lin Feng wrapped his fingers around its insubstantial hilt, he felt it solid beneath his grip.

"I did it," he whispered.

"You have taken the first true step on the Celestial Blade path. This is not a technique, boy. This is your soul, given edge. Guard it. Nurture it. One day, when you are strong enough, that blade will cut through mountains."

The silver sword flickered and faded. Lin Feng collapsed to his knees, utterly drained. But the memory of it remained — the memory of holding something that was purely, completely his.

"Rest now," the Sword God said. "Tomorrow, we begin the Second Form. The Sect Tournament is in three months. If you intend to compete — and I assure you, you do — you will need every moment between now and then."

Lin Feng lay back on the cold stone of the cave floor, staring up at the phosphorescent moss. Three months to transform from the weakest disciple in the sect into something that could stand on a tournament stage.

"You're insane," he thought.

"Yes," the Sword God agreed. "That's rather the point."

And somewhere in the darkness of the cave, Lin Feng smiled.
